content:
I used to think landing a software engineering job was all about learning another programming language. Looking back, I realized it was more about knowing where to learn, what to practice, and how to stay consistent.

15 min read
Just now

--

When I first decided that I wanted to become a software engineer, I did what most beginners do. I searched for “best programming language to learn,” watched countless YouTube videos, bookmarked dozens of websites, and enrolled in multiple online courses. Every creator seemed to have a different roadmap. One person insisted that mastering Data Structures and Algorithms was enough. Another claimed projects were all that mattered. Someone else argued that system design should be the first priority. The more advice I consumed, the more confused I became. Instead of moving forward, I spent weeks trying to figure out which resource I should trust.
Eventually, I stopped searching for the perfect roadmap and started paying attention to something much simpler. I looked at developers who had already landed jobs at companies I admired. Whether they worked at startups, product companies, or large tech organizations, I noticed a pattern. They weren’t using hundreds of different resources. Instead, they consistently relied on a handful of websites that solved different parts of the interview journey. One…
